coredata.dc:title: H2O2 self-supplying Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme with NIR light enhanced catalytic activity and photothermal synergistic antibacterial application
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Feijian
Yang, Dezhi
Xun, Chun
Li, Haiyan
Li, Qiulan
Zhong, Zitao
Wei, Daqiao
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes have drawn increasing attention due to unique enzyme-mimic catalytic activities for the treatment of bacterial infections. Herein, a novel tri-metal nanozyme for synergistic eradication of Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) was generated by Mo/Fe doped carbon dots (Mo/Fe-CDs) wrapping copper peroxide (CuO2) nanozyme (Mo/Fe@CuO2). Mo/Fe@CuO2 showed near-infrared (NIR) light-enhanced peroxidase-like catalytic activity, and H2O2 self-supply to promote the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) for highly efficient antibacterial photothermal therapy. The CuO2 could generate H2O2 in the acidic microenvironment, and Mo/Fe-CDs consequently could convert H2O2 to hydroxyl radicals (·OH), resulting in the improvement of sterilization efficiency. Interestingly, Mo/Fe-CDs could activate the transformation of Cu2+ to Cu+ through the co-catalytic reaction based on the Mo4+/Mo6+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ redox couples, and provided photonic hyperthermia (PTT) to enhance the peroxidase-like activity. Meanwhile, Mo/Fe@CuO2 showed glutathione (GSH) oxidase-like activity, which can achieve the depletion of glutathione (GSH) in bacteria, thus significantly improving the bactericidal effect. The collaborative eradication of bacteria, through innovative metal carbon-based nanozyme technology, provides a powerful and feasible approach to treating bacterial infections that threaten human health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozyme
H2O2 self-supplying
Peroxidase-like activity
Photocatalytic
Synergistic antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of bacterial samples for SEM
Steady-state kinetics and POD-like catalytic mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Kunming University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was greatly supported by Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202201BE070001-032 and 202201AU070117 ), Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2020M20202118090 and 2021T20200097 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images: (A) CuO2, (B) Mo/Fe-CDs, (C) Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanocomposites; (D) HRTEM images of Mo/Fe@CuO2; (E) The XRD pattern of Mo/Fe-CDs; (F) FT-IR spectra of the different materials; (G) XPS spectra of Mo/Fe, CuO2 and Mo/Fe@CuO2; (H) Mo 3d and (I) Cu 2p spectra of the different nanozymes.

(A) The UV–vis spectrum of (a) TMB, (b) Mo/Fe + TMB, (c) CuO2 + TMB, (d) Mo/Fe@CuO2 + TMB and (e) Mo/Fe@CuO2 + TMB + NIR system; (B) POD-like activity of Mo/Fe@CuO2 with OPD, TMB and ABTS, respectively; (C) The effect of time and H2O2 on the POD-like activity of Mo/Fe@CuO2; (D) The steady-state kinetic analysis of the Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme with TMB as substrate. (E) The steady-state kinetic analysis of the Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme under NIR irritation. (F) The fluorescent spectra of ADHP reaction system (G) The UV–vis spectrum of the MB reaction system. (H) Loss of GSH plot after incubation with Mo/Fe, CuO2 and Mo/Fe@CuO2 for 3 h.

(A) The temperature change curves of the three materials under 808 nm laser irradiation for 10 min; (B) Temperature curves of Mo/Fe@CuO2 with different concentrations under 808 nm irradiation. (C) Mo/Fe@CuO2 was irradiated by the NIR laser until the temperature change was stabilized, followed by natural cooling to room temperature. (D) Linear fitting of time vs the negative natural logarithm of driving force temperature (θ) from the cooling period.

In vitro antibacterial performance: (A) Images of E. coli colonies after treating with various nanomaterials; (B) Relative bacterial viability was calculated by counting the E. coli colonies in (A). (C) Images of S. aureus colonies after treating with various nanomaterials; (D) Relative bacterial viability of was calculated by counting the S. aureus colonies in (C) ((n = 3), **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001).

(A) Fluorescence images of E. coli and S. aureus after treated with different groups: Control, Mo/Fe, CuO2, Mo/Fe@CuO2, Mo/Fe@CuO2 + NIR; (B–C) E. coli and S. aureus stained by CV after treatment with different materials treatments; (D) ROS production of Mo/Fe, CuO2 and Mo/Fe@CuO2 under 808 nm NIR light irradiation as determined by DCFH-DA.
SEM morphology of bacteria treated with Mo/Fe@CuO2.
Synthesis and antibacterial application of Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme with POD-like activity and H2O2 self-supplying capacity.

head.ce:title: H2O2 self-supplying Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme with NIR light enhanced catalytic activity and photothermal synergistic antibacterial application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, Yunnan Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Salt Industry Co., Ltd., Kunming 650000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunnan Salt Industry Co., Ltd., Kunming 650000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medicine, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, Yunnan Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medicine, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, Yunnan Province, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozymes have drawn increasing attention due to unique enzyme-mimic catalytic activities for the treatment of bacterial infections. Herein, a novel tri-metal nanozyme for synergistic eradication of Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) was generated by Mo/Fe doped carbon dots (Mo/Fe-CDs) wrapping copper peroxide (CuO2) nanozyme (Mo/Fe@CuO2). Mo/Fe@CuO2 showed near-infrared (NIR) light-enhanced peroxidase-like catalytic activity, and H2O2 self-supply to promote the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) for highly efficient antibacterial photothermal therapy. The CuO2 could generate H2O2 in the acidic microenvironment, and Mo/Fe-CDs consequently could convert H2O2 to hydroxyl radicals (·OH), resulting in the improvement of sterilization efficiency. Interestingly, Mo/Fe-CDs could activate the transformation of Cu2+ to Cu+ through the co-catalytic reaction based on the Mo4+/Mo6+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ redox couples, and provided photonic hyperthermia (PTT) to enhance the peroxidase-like activity. Meanwhile, Mo/Fe@CuO2 showed glutathione (GSH) oxidase-like activity, which can achieve the depletion of glutathione (GSH) in bacteria, thus significantly improving the bactericidal effect. The collaborative eradication of bacteria, through innovative metal carbon-based nanozyme technology, provides a powerful and feasible approach to treating bacterial infections that threaten human health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the biggest risks to human health is bacterial infection, which causes a great deal of morbidity and mortality in people all over the world [1,2]. As common bacteriostat, long-term use of antibiotics is easy to cause bacterial resistance and greatly reduce the therapeutic effect [3,4]. Urgent action must be taken to develop different approach to effectively kill drug-resistant bacteria [5]. Nanozyme is a type of nanomaterial with inherent enzyme mimicking activity and fascinating enzyme catalytic properties that can inactivate bacteria by promoting the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [6–8]. In addition, photodynamic therapy (PDT), photothermal therapy (PTT) and chemodynamic therapy (CDT) based on nanozymes have attracted most attention due to their ability to overcome the growing problem of bacterial resistance [9–12]. The PTT exploits local near-infrared (NIR) irritation to produce higher temperature for killing bacterial [13,14]. Under visible or near-infrared light irradiation, PDT achieves the bactericidal effect by generating more ROS, including hydroxyl radical (•OH), singlet oxygen radical (1O2) and superoxide anion radical (•O2 −) [15,16]. Nanozyme with peroxidase (POD)-like activity can catalyze H2O2 molecules around the site of infection, initiating a chemical reaction that produces antibacterial ROS, thereby triggering lipid peroxidation in bacterial cells [17–19]. Nevertheless, the intrinsic catalytic activity of nanozyme has relatively insufficient antibacterial properties [20]. Furthermore, the antibacterial activity of CDT is severely constrained by the infectious microenvironments, such low oxygen level and excessive glutathione (GSH) expression [21–23]. The combination of photoactivation and nanozymatic strategy is expected to be a candidate for antimicrobial therapy [24]. In recent years, several nano-metals (such as Cu, Mo, Fe, Mn, etc.) with mimic enzyme and photothermal properties have been studied and applied to improve therapeutic outcome of antibacterial treatment [25,26]. As emerging type of bactericidal materials, Cu, Fe and Mo-involved nanomaterials have numerous potential prospect. All of polyvalent transition metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+ and Mo6+) could mediate the redox reaction for CDT, NIR absorption and photothermal conversion performance [27].

In this work, the novel Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme was designed by Mo/Fe (the abbreviation of Mo/Fe-CDs) wrapped with copper peroxide nanodots. The obtained hybrid nanozyme showed excellent photothermal conversion and photo-enhanced POD-like properties. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , CuO2 possesses a high capacity of self-supplying H2O2 in acidic microenvironment, and then Mo/Fe further decomposes H2O2 to produce more ·OH. In addition, Mo/Fe@CuO2 could deplete GSH capability under neutral condition, and then the antibacterial activity of Mo/Fe@Cu2O could enhanced by NIR light. In vitro experiments, the result exhibited that Mo/Fe@CuO2 could efficiently eradicate Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) under NIR light irradiation. The mechanism of synergistic antibacterial action of CDT and PTT on bacteria had also been systematically studied.

All chemicals are analytical grade without any further purification. Copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O), ethylenediamine (EDA), sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), dopamine (DA), sodium acetate (CH3COONa), Amplex Red (ADHP) 5,5′-Dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) and ethylene glycol (C2H6O2) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). The E. coli (ATCC 8090) and S. aureus (ATCC 6538) were obtained from China Microbial Strain Center. Propidium iodide (PI) and SYTO 9 were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All solutions were prepared with deionized water (DI, ≥18.2 MΩ).

The Na2MoO4·2H2O (1.192 g), 1.331 g of FeCl3·6H2O, 0.040 g of DA, 2.1 g of NaAc and 50 μL of EDA were completely added to 30 mL of deionized water and magnetically stirred for 5 min. Then, the as-prepared solution was transferred into a 100 mL reaction Teflon-lined autoclave and then kept at 200 °C for 12 h. Finally, the solution of Mo/Fe was filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane, and the underlying solid was completely washed with water and ethanol. Then the obtained particle was labeled as Mo/Fe.[28].

CuO2 was prepared by using a reported method [29]. Specifically, 2 g of PVP was added into 20 mL of CuCl2·2H2O (0.2 M) aqueous solution. After that, 4 mL of H2O2 (30 %) and 4 mL of NaOH (1 M) aqueous solution were successively added into the above solution. After starring for 30 min, high-speed centrifugation was used to segregate the mixture. The obtained solid nanomaterial was washed three times by deionized water. Finally, the CuO2 was collected and dried at 60 °C for 8 h in a vacuum.

For the synthesis of Mo/Fe@CuO2 composites, 5 mL of Mo/Fe (1 mg/mL) and 5 mL of CuO2 (1 mg/mL) were added into a 10 mL deionized water. Subsequently, all kinds of mixed solution were magnetically stirred for 10 h in room temperature. The final solution is labelled as Mo/Fe@CuO2.

Typically, the POD-like catalytic activity of Mo/Fe@CuO2 was measured by catalytic oxidation of the chromogenic substrates (TMB, OPD and ABTS). In detail, 100 μL of Mo/Fe@CuO2 suspension (1 mg/mL) and 200 μL of substrate (5 mM) were successively added into the centrifugal tube. Subsequently, all solutions were diluted to 3 mL by using NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 4.0, 0.2 M). Then the solution absorbance was recorded by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Finally, the NIR group was exposed at 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2), and the absorbance was recorded by UV–vis.

Initially, the photothermal heating properties of various nanoparticle solutions were examined by monitoring the temperature changes. The 3 mL of mixed solutions containing CuO2 (100 µg/mL), Mo/Fe-CDs (100 µg/mL) and Mo/Fe@CuO2 (100 µg/mL) were irradiated by 808 nm NIR laser (1.5 W/cm2). The solution temperature was recorded by a digital thermometer every 60 s. In order to investigate the Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme photothermal performance, the different concentrations of nanozyme were added and irradiated by using the 808 nm NIR Laser (1.5 W/cm2) for 10 min. Moreover, the Mo/Fe@CuO2 photothermal stability was measured by repeatedly heating 4 times under near-infrared laser irradiation and then natural cooling to room temperature. Finally, the Mo/Fe@CuO2 photothermal conversion efficiency was determined by reported method [30].

It is well known that ADHP as a specific hydrogen peroxide fluorescent probe can react with H2O2 to produce a strong red fluorescent substance (Excitation wavelength: 571 nm, Emission wavelength: 585 nm). The H2O2 produced by Mo/Fe, CuO2 and Mo/Fe@CuO2 (100 μg/mL) were determined by the fluorescent probe, respecctively. MB degradation-based colorimetric assay was used to investigate •OH generation by nanozyme. Additionally, 200 μL of Mo/Fe, CuO2 and Mo/Fe@CuO2 suspension were incubated in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0 and containing 20 μL of MB) for 4 h, respectively. At this moment, the mixture was determined at 665 nm using a UV–Vis absorption spectrophotometer. The DTNB chromogen was used to estimate the GSH-diminishing ability of Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozymes, which could generate the yellow product by reacting with GSH. In detail, 300 μL of Mo/Fe@CuO2 (1 mg/mL) was added into the solution of 5 mL of GSH (1 mM), then incubating for 3 h. Then, 20 μL of DTNB (1 mM) was added to 100 μL mixed solution and reacted for 15 min. Finally, the solution absorbance was measured at 412 nm to evaluate the GSH depletion ability.

The E. coli and S. aureus bacteria (2 × 105 CFU/mL) were treated with PBS, Mo/Fe, CuO2 and Mo/Fe@CuO2 (100 μg/mL), respectively. The NIR group was exposed at 808 nm NIR for 10 min. All groups were incubated for 2 h in dark, and then 5 μM of ROS probe (DCFH-DA) was added to react for 20 min. The processed bacteria solution was washed by using PBS for 3 times. Finally, the fluorescence was observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (λex = 485 nm, λem = 545 nm).

E. coli (ATCC 8090) and S. aureus (ATCC 6538) were selected as the model to evaluate the antibacterial performance of Mo/Fe@CuO2. 100 μL of bacterial suspension (2 × 107 CFU/mL) mixture and 100 μL of Mo/Fe@CuO2 (1 mg/mL) were incubated for 3 h. Then, the suspensions were diluted and injected on culture plates for incubation. The bacteria samples were treated with PBS, Mo/Fe, CuO2, Mo/Fe@CuO2, Mo/Fe + NIR, CuO2 + NIR, Mo/Fe@CuO2 + NIR, respectively.

Live and dead bacteria were stained with PI and SYTO 9. Firstly, bacterial suspensions from different treatment groups were centrifuged at 4000 rpm and washed with sterile water. 10 μL of PI (5 μg/mL) and 10 μL of SYTO-9 (5 μg/mL) were progressively mixed with bacteria solution in dark for 30 min. The stained bacteria from all the treatment groups were washed with PBS, and finally imaged under a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of bacterial samples for SEM
ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria continuously grown from a single colony were introduced into fresh LB medium and incubated at 37 °C in a shaking incubator. Following nanocomposites treatment, the bacteria solutions were washed three times with PBS. 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution was used to fix the bacterial cells at 4 °C overnight. The cleaned cells were then put onto single-crystal silicon wafers and dehydrated with methanol.

The bacterial suspension was transferred to 24-well plates and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h with Mo/Fe, CuO2 and Mo/Fe@CuO2, respectively. The bacterial biofilms were gently sucked out after washing by PBS two or three times. After that, the bacterial biofilms was exposure to the blank buffer, and 100 μL of 33 % methanol was added to dissolve the biofilms. Subsequently, 1 % CV solution was added to the wells and reacted about 15 min. Lastly, the absorbance at 590 nm was measured.

MTT was used to evaluate the nanozymes cytotoxicity. HT29 cell was employed to assess the cytotoxicity of Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozymes. In briefly, 96-well plates were used to incubate HT29 cell, which had a cell density of 105 cells per mL, for 24 h in a 37 °C incubator with 5 % CO2. Then, different concentrations of Mo/Fe@CuO2 (0∼300 µg/mL) were incubated for 24 h and 48 h. After washing the cells twice with PBS, the mixture was added into 50 L of MTT solution. The mixture was then incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. As the color changes, 200 μL of DMSO was added into each well, and the residual culture medium was removed by gently shaking it for 15 min. A microplate reader was used to measure the absorbance at 590 nm.

Error bars represents the standard deviations for different parallel experiments. Student's t test was used to determine the significance of experimental groups. Differences between different groups at *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 were considered as statistically significant.

The morphologies of the as-prepared nanozymes were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A and Fig. S1, the synthesized CuO2 presents a fusiformis sheet like structure. The synthesized Mo/Fe shows a regular round particle structure (Fig. 1B and Fig. S2). And the Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme displays the irregular sheet like structure (Fig. 1C and Fig. S3). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image was depicted in Fig. 1D, two different sizes of lattice fringes were clearly observed. The lattice spaces of 0.28 nm and 0.45 nm were attributed to the (110) plane of CuO2 and (111) crystal plane of Fe3O4, respectively. All those results firmly confirmed that it is closely followed the XRD data.

XRD patterns were studied to identify the phases of the synthesized particles. The XRD pattern for Mo/Fe@CuO2 (Fig. 1E) shows peaks at angles of 2θ 23.5°, 28.3°, 32.1°, 37.0°, 44.5°, 53.3°, 64.2° and 66.9°. The diffraction peaks at 23.5°, 28.3°, 37.0° and 53.3°are corresponded to the standard card for CuO2 (JCPDS: 65-9026). The other peaks were consistent with the Mo and Fe lattice spacing [31]. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy is employed to investigate the surface chemical groups of Mo/Fe@CuO2, and the spectra of materials are shown in Fig. 1F. The characteristic peaks at 816 cm−1 and 3412 cm−1 and 1636 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of CC bond, CONH bond and CO bond (amide), respectively. The characteristic peaks at 1436 cm−1 and 1368 cm−1 are the stretching vibration of CN bond and CH bond, respectively [32]. The peaks at 509 cm−1 and 558 cm−1 explained the presence of CuO and FeO [33,34]. These results indicated the successful synthesis of the Mo/Fe@CuO2.

XPS was carried out to analyze the composition and valence states of Mo/Fe@CuO2. As indicated in Fig. 1G, the peaks indexed to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, Fe 2p, Cu 2p and Mo 3d are clearly observed. High-resolution XPS spectra illustrated that the spectrum of C1s spectrum can be fitted to the 284.3 eV peak, demonstrating the existence of CC and CC bonds (Fig. S4A).Two pairs of peaks at 952.24 and 932.43 eV in Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. S4B) can be attributed to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 of Cu+ and Cu2+ [35]. As shown in Fig. 1H, the appearance of peaks at 234.17 and 230.88 eV are attributed to Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2, respectively. These binding energy values also suggested the predominant presence of Mo4+, and other two peaks (235.05 and 231.90 eV) were ascribed to Mo6+ [36,37]. The peaks at binding energies of 724.38 and 710.96 eV are characteristic peaks of Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 in Mo/Fe@CuO2 (Fig. 1I), revealing the existence of Fe2+ [38,39]. The results confirmed that the mixed valence states (Cu+/Cu2+, Fe2+/Fe3+ and Mo4+/Mo6+) were existed in Mo/Fe@CuO2.

To systematically demonstrate the POD-like activities of nanocomposites, TMB colorimetric probe was used to measure and optimize the various reaction conditions. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the CuO2 or Mo/Fe-CDs alone can catalyze TMB form little blue solution, indicating the weak catalytic activity. In contrast, Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanocomposites could cause the colorless TMB to produce deep blue oxTMB production in acidic condition. When the reaction solution was exposed under the NIR laser (808 nm, 10 min), the absorbance drastically rises by comparing to the control groups. The NIR could improve POD-like activity of Mo/Fe@CuO2, whereas CuO2 or Mo/Fe-CDs alone only possessed weak catalytic activity. In addition, Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme could also react with two other chromogenic substrates (i.e. neutral OPD and negatively charged ABTS) (Fig. 2B), indicating that the catalytic activity of Mo/Fe@CuO2 is independent of the substrate employed. The Mo/Fe@CuO2 could catalyze TMB, ABTS and OPD to form colored production, and the absorption peaks were situated at 654, 421 and 721 nm, respectively. Consequently, the results demonstrated that Mo/Fe@CuO2 has extremely intrinsic POD-like activity, and the catalytic activity was improved significantly by NIR irritation.

The factors affecting the activity of nanozymes were also studied, including pH, time and temperature. As shown in Fig. 2C, the oxTMB formation is gradually increased with the reaction time in acidic environment. In the presence of endogenous H2O2, the absorbance of reaction system was increased firstly and then remained unchanged. After adding exogenous H2O2, the absorbance of reaction solution was increased continuously. The result proved that the H2O2 self-supply capacity of Mo/Fe@CuO2 is limited. With the passage of time, the production rate of oxTMB gradually slowed down, and the catalytic efficiency also was gradually decreased. Since the majority of nanozymes have outstanding catalytic activity in slightly acidic circumstances, the buffer pH effect was investigated. When the pH was 4.0 (Fig. S5A), the absorbance was maximum at the wavelength of 654 nm, indicating that the content of oxTMB in the system was highest. In acidic environment, CuO2 could react with H+ to produce H2O2, and the Mo/Fe nanozyme catalyzed H2O2 to generate more ·OH. Additionally, The POD-like activity of the Mo/Fe@CuO2 was also measured at different temperatures. The Mo/Fe@CuO2 still showed high activity after incubation at 60 °C (Fig. S5B), indicating that the Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme are resistant to relatively high temperatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetics and POD-like catalytic mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the catalytic activity of POD-like process, the apparent steady-state kinetic factors were measured under the reaction system (pH = 4.0, 25 °C).

As shown in Fig. 2D, the Michaelis-Menten and Lineweaver-Burk curves of Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme were obtained. According to the Michaelis-Menten plots, the hyperbolic fitting parameters were calculated, and the Km value is 2.48 mM and Vmax is 0.28 × 10−6 M·s−1. Under the NIR irrational conditions (Fig. 2E), the K
m and V
max were calculated to be 0.90 mM and 3.45 × 10−6 M·s−1. Under NIR irritation, Km value is lower and Vmax value is higher than without treatments. This result proved that NIR irradiation could improve the affinity between nanoenzyme and substrate, and the maximum reaction rate was increased significantly. Compared with previous reported literatures (Table S1), Mo/Fe@CuO2 indicated the same affinity and higher reaction rate.

To verify the productive abilities of H2O2, a fluorescent probe (Amplex Red) was used to sensitively capture H2O2 to form red fluorescent products with a peak at 585 nm. Fig. 2F shows that the Mo/Fe@CuO2 group has a higher fluorescence signal by comparing to Mo/Fe and CuO2 groups. The Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme showed the self-supplying H2O2 activity, which is mainly due to the catalytic formation of H2O2 by CuO2 in acidic environment. As shown in the Fig. 2G, the absorbance is significantly decreased under the Mo/Fe@CuO2, indicating that Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme can produce plentiful ·OH due to H2O2 self-supplying and Mo/Fe co-catalytic activity. As a powerful natural antioxidant, glutathione (GSH) is essential for maintaining the balance of redox inside of cells. We characterized the GSH oxidase-like activity of Mo/Fe@CuO2 by using 5,5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) as a GSH-specific indicator. Compared with the single material, a more negligible characteristic absorption peak was showed with the addition of Mo/Fe@CuO2, and it revealed that glutathione level was reduced significantly (Fig. 2H). This demonstrated that Mo/Fe@CuO2 could oxidize GSH in the presence of oxygen rather than Mo/Fe and CuO2.

The photothermal measurement experiment of Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme was investigated by exposing to 808 nm laser, the solution temperature variation was recorded. As shown in Fig. 3 A, temperature is increasing markedly under 808 nm laser irradiation for 10 min in the group of Mo/Fe, CuO2 and Mo/Fe@CuO2. Compared with the CuO2 group, the temperature of Mo/Fe@CuO2 group only has slightly improvement. Therefore, it can be concluded that the CuO2 has great contribution to the photothermal conversion performance of Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme. When the Mo/Fe@CuO2 concentration gradually increases from 3 to 100 µg/mL, the aqueous solution temperature rises from 51.8 to 67.2 °C under the 808 nm laser irradiation for long time (Fig. 3B). In general, the relationship of temperature is directly proportional to the concentration of Mo/Fe@CuO2 in time duration of light exposure. To explored the photothermal performance of Mo/Fe@CuO2, we calculated that the photothermal conversion efficiency was 36.3 % by the heating and cooling curves (Fig. 3C–3D). Furthermore, the Mo/Fe@CuO2 photostability was measured by four cycles of laser on/off processes (Fig. S6), and the heating–cooling curves of all cycles showed minimal variations, demonstrating the material displayed remarkable photostability. These results showed that the prepared Mo/Fe@CuO2 has great photothermal performance, which could be used as a superb platform PTT candidate for killing bacteria.

Agar plate counting test was used to evaluate the antibacterial activities of Mo/Fe@CuO2 against E. coli and S. aureus. As shown in Fig. 4 A–D, the control and Mo/Fe groups are contained a large number of bacterial colonies, indicating that Mo/Fe possessed a negligible effect on the bacterial growth. The CuO2 and Mo/Fe@CuO2 exhibited strong bactericidal effect without NIR irradiation. Compared with CuO2, Mo/Fe@CuO2 significantly reduced the survival of E. coli (3.6 %) and S. aureus (4.8 %) at the same concentration. The results demonstrated that Mo/Fe co-catalysis could be applied to improve the CDT efficacy by producing more ·OH [23]. Although the Mo/Fe@CuO2 group was able to remove most bacteria, it was not fully sufficient to treat bacterial infections. When the Mo/Fe@CuO2 was exposed to 808 nm NIR laser irradiation, the bacterial survival rate is significantly decreased. All the results indicated that the Mo/Fe@CuO2 possessed high photothermal effect and POD-like catalytic activity for eradicating bacteria.

The distribution of live/dead bacterial were stained by SYTO-9 and PI, and it could be seen clearly under confocal fluorescence microscope. The live bacteria can be stained with SYTO-9 and display green fluorescence, while the death bacteria exhibit red fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 5 A, a lot of red fluorescent signs can be observed in the Mo/Fe@CuO2 group, suggesting that numerous bacteria were killed by high level ROS. Under the NIR irradiation, the red fluorescent signs (dead bacteria) significantly increased, which was consistent with the results of previous plate counting experiments. These results further confirmed that Mo/Fe@CuO2 exhibit excellent antibacterial ability by CDT and PTT. Crystal violet (CV) staining method was used to evaluate the antibiofilm performance of Mo/Fe@CuO2. As shown in Fig. 5B–C, the dark purple was observed in the E. coli and S. aureus control group, indicating the formation of a nearly complete biofilm. In contrast, the Mo/Fe@CuO2 group displayed an almost clear purple color, and the absorbance was significantly lower than that of other groups. This result revealed that Mo/Fe@CuO2 could disrupt the formation of biofilms and limit bacterial proliferation. Usually, the bacterial membrane structure is destroyed, leading to leakage of intracellular proteins, while could achieve antibacterial activity.

To investigate the mechanism of antibacterial activity, the ROS generation was investigated by incubating different treatments bacteria with florescent probe DCFH-DA. As shown in Fig. 5D, a significant number of green fluorescence is observed in the Mo/Fe@CuO2 group under 808 nm laser irradiation, which confirmed that Mo/Fe@CuO2 can be used to induce a large quantity of ROS through the photocatalytic and POD-like activity under NIR irradiation. In order to further investigate the antibacterial mechanism of Mo/Fe@CuO2, SEM was used to characterize the morphological changes E. coli and S. aureus after treatment with Mo/Fe@CuO2. The result is shown in Fig. 6 , and the bacteria surface is full and smooth without treatment, but the completeness of the bacterial cell membrane is significantly destroyed in presence of Mo/Fe@CuO2, resulting in the bacterial surface breakdown or destruction. In order to assess the biotoxicity of the Mo/Fe@CuO2, the MTT method was used to measure the effect of Mo/Fe@CuO2 on the viability of HT29 cells. Specifically, HT29 cells were treated with Mo/Fe@CuO2 at different concentrations (0–300 μg/mL) and incubated for 48 h (Fig. S7). The results showed that the survival rate of HT29 cells could reach 95.68 % after 48 h of incubation by the Mo/Fe@CuO2 (300 μg/mL), thus indicating that the Mo/Fe@CuO2 has excellent biocompatibility.

In conclusion, Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme was successfully constructed, which are applied for the antibacterial treatment by CDT and PTT. Mo/Fe@CuO2 showed excellent POD-like activity and antibacterial activity under NIR 808 nm irradiation. Meanwhile, CuO2 displayed the ability of H2O2 self-supplying in acidic environment, and then Mo/Fe catalyzed H2O2 to generate more ·OH for CDT. Importantly, the Mo/Fe@CuO2 nanozyme possessed good biocompatibility, high photothermal conversion efficiency, and excellent self-supplying H2O2 capability. Under the combination of CDT/PTT, E. coli and S. aureus survival rate was reduced to 0.05 % after Mo/Fe@CuO2 treatment. The work not only provides the paradigm for fabricating metal peroxide nanomaterials, but also presents a new strategy to improve CDT and PTT efficacy. In summary, this work offers a fresh approach to future multimode nano-antibiotic development.

This work was greatly supported by Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202201BE070001-032 and 202201AU070117), Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2020M20202118090 and 2021T20200097).

Feijian Xiao: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Dezhi Yang: Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Chun Xun: Validation, Data curation. Haiyan Li: Investigation, Data curation, Software. Qiulan Li: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Zitao Zhong: Validation, Supervision. Daqiao Wei: Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yaling Yang: Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.158862.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
The Supporting Information is available free of charge at instrumentation, additional TEM and XPS spectra, conditional experiments, Temperature fluctuation of Mo/Fe@CuO2, cell viability and kinetic parameters.
